task.name: STOP-IT (jsPsych Stop Signal Task)
task.description: Stop-signal task. White left/right arrows require a corresponding arrow-key response on go trials. On stop-signal trials (~25%), the white arrow turns red after a variable stop-signal delay (SSD, starting at 200 ms, staircased by ±50 ms toward 50% commission accuracy); the participant must withhold the response. 1 practice block (32 trials) + 4 experimental blocks (64 trials each). MAXRT = 1250 ms from stimulus onset.

stimuli.description: Red leftward arrow (stop signal overlaying the go_left arrow)
Red rightward arrow (stop signal overlaying the go_right arrow)
White leftward arrow with black border (go trial; correct response = ArrowLeft)
White rightward arrow with black border (go trial; correct response = ArrowRight)

phase_detection.complete: !!document.querySelector('#jspsych-download-as-text-link') || ((document.body.textContent || '').includes('Thank you for your participation') && (typeof jsPsych !== 'undefined') && jsPsych.progress().percent_complete >= 100)
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { const t = document.body.textContent || ''; return t.includes('GO TRIALS:') || t.includes('STOP-SIGNAL TRIALS:') || t.includes('correct response') || t.includes('incorrect response') || t.includes('correct stop') || t.includes('remember: try to stop') || t.includes('too slow') || t.includes('too fast') || t.includes('Press space to begin') || t.includes('Get ready') || t.includes('Thank you for your participation'); })()
phase_detection.practice: (typeof jsPsych !== 'undefined') && (() => { const v = jsPsych.data.get().filter({trial_type: 'custom-stop-signal-plugin'}).values(); return v.length > 0 && v[v.length - 1].block_i === 0; })()

timing.response_window_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('img#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!img) return false; const src = img.src || ''; return src.includes('go_') || src.includes('stop_'); })()
timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('img#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!img) return ''; const src = img.src || ''; const m = src.match(/(fix|go_left|go_right|stop_left|stop_right)/); return m ? m[1] : ''; })()

pilot_validation_config.rationale: The practice block runs NdesignReps_practice (=4) repetitions of the basic 8-trial design = 32 trials with expected proportions go_left:go_right:stop ≈ 3:3:2. 16 trials ensures all three conditions are observed at least once with very high probability while keeping the pilot brief; the practice block is the natural single-block scope (block_i == 0) and yields trial-by-trial feedback that helps surface selector/timing issues.

_reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'STOP-IT (jsPsych Stop Signal Task)' with 4 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 3 conditions; enabled 3 temporal effects.

go_left.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard family for stop-signal go RTs (Matzke & Wagenmakers, Heathcote). For healthy young adults in STOP-IT-style arrow tasks, mean go RT is ~530-550 ms; partitioning into mu~430, sigma~40, tau~110 yields a mean near 540 ms with right-skew consistent with published fits. Left/right go conditions are symmetric, so identical parameters.

go_right.rationale: Symmetric with go_left; no published evidence for left/right asymmetry in stop-signal arrow paradigms. Mean ~540 ms, sigma/tau as for go_left.

stop.rationale: Failed-stop (signal-respond) RTs are faster than matched go RTs by ~40-70 ms — a core race-model prediction (Logan & Cowan 1984; Verbruggen & Logan 2009). Shifting mu down ~60 ms and slightly compressing tau yields mean ~465 ms, consistent with reported signal-respond RT < mean go RT in healthy adults.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation is a general property of speeded-choice tasks (Wagenmakers, Farrell & Ratcliff 2004; Gilden 2001). Phi~0.15 reflects the short-range positive dependency typically observed in stop-signal go RTs; values >0.25 are rare.

fatigue_drift.rationale: STOP-IT is brief (~280 trials, ~10 min) with frequent breaks between blocks. Verbruggen et al. (2019) report stable mean RT across the 4 experimental blocks; a monotonic per-trial drift is not the dominant temporal structure here. Practice (block-level) and post-event slowing capture the documented dynamics.

condition_repetition.rationale: Repetition priming for arrow direction in stop-signal is not consistently reported as a primary effect in this paradigm; SSD staircasing and stop-signal proportion are the dominant trial-level structure. Leaving disabled avoids introducing unwarranted modulation.

pink_noise.rationale: Long-range 1/f structure in RT exists (Gilden 2001) but overlaps substantially with the enabled autocorrelation mechanism at this trial count (~280). To avoid double-counting short-range temporal dependency, only the AR(1) mechanism is enabled.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Stop-signal does not have a documented condition-sequence interaction analogous to congruency-sequence in conflict tasks. The well-documented sequence effects (post-error slowing, post-stop slowing) are event-triggered and handled by post_event_slowing, not by transition-table modulation.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Two distinct event-triggered slowing effects are documented for stop-signal: (a) classical post-error slowing on the trial after a go-trial error (~30-60 ms; Rabbitt 1966; Dutilh et al. 2012), and (b) post-stop-signal slowing on the trial after any stop trial, regardless of stop outcome (~40-90 ms; Bissett & Logan 2011; Verbruggen, Logan, Liefooghe & Vandierendonck 2008). The interrupt trigger is marked exclusive_with_prior_triggers so a stop trial that the bot failed to inhibit does not double-add the error and interrupt slowing.

practice_effect.rationale: STOP-IT participants typically speed up ~30-60 ms from the early practice/first-experimental block to asymptote, plateauing by the 2nd-3rd experimental block (Verbruggen et al. 2019; general stop-signal practice literature). Initial offset of +45 ms decaying to zero by block 2 reflects this without overstating; decay_rate 0.55 yields most of the speedup in the first block.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: STOP-IT is short and block-structured with explicit breaks; vigilance decrement (increasing variability over sustained uninterrupted attention) is documented for longer continuous tasks (e.g., PVT, SART), not for stop-signal of this length. Leaving disabled.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in healthy young adults on stop-signal is ~60-80 ms (Verbruggen et al. 2019 normative data; Congdon et al. 2012). Condition-level SD (~25 ms) captures the spread in the signal-respond minus go RT contrast across subjects. sigma_tau multiplicative jitter of ±15% reflects published variability in ex-Gaussian fit parameters across individuals. Go-trial accuracy is consistently >95% in healthy adults so accuracy_sd 0.03 with floor at 0.80 is conservative; omission rate is near-zero so SD 0.01 with cap at 0.05 is appropriate.
